Caption: 2D Magnetic Resonance images of a cellulose paste at different times during drying. Note the « tripod » (cubic volume filled with water) lying at a given distance over the sample free surface, and providing a reference to determine, despite shrinkage, the effective sample surface. The NMR signal on the color scale (in arbitrary units) is for each phase an increasing, but non-linear, function of the local water amount. Data from Ref. [32].

Scheme of the first setup, with geometry (I) (see text). Some of the thermocouples along a vertical plane going through the central axis are shown by horizontal green lines, but the exact positions of all thermocouples are shown in the inset of Fig. 5a. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Scheme of the second setup, with geometry (II) (see text).

Arrangement of the thermocouples inside the sample of geometry (II). Two thermocouples at the top and bottom of the specimen are directly attached to its surface; the remaining 6 thermocouples inside the specimen are arranged alternately and layered to avoid damage to the specimen by the drill bit resulting from too close holes (see text).

Temperature evolution over time at the different depths inside the material (see inset of (a)) for an air flux at 20 L/min and an initial sample temperature of 60°. In (b), we show the temperature evolution at different times as a function of the height along the sample axis.

Caption: Conductivity correction factor as a function of the wall aspect ratio to get a 1D flow in the central region, for different ratios of the boundary conditions (see text).
